coredata.dc:title: Highly selective histamine assay via SERS: Based on the signal enhancement of carbon dots and the fluorescence quenching of gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dezhi
Li, Hong
Li, Qiulan
Li, Kexiang
Xiao, Feijian
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent background is a major obstacle in surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and limits its application in various fields of science. This research developed a novel strategy by combining the advantages of the carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to increase the SERS signal for histamine (His) detection. The β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) decorated N,Zn co-doped carbon dots (N,Zn-CDs/β-CD) was prepared, which has both highly selective fluorescence (FL) sensitization and selective Raman signal enhancement for His derivatives (D-His). The prepared AuNPs not only could be used to enhance SERS signal, but also could quench 76% of the FL background at 544 nm, which is conducive to suppress the FL interference caused by carbon dots. Interestingly, the SERS signal increased with the gradual quenching of FL through the energy transfer between the fluorophore and the AuNPs. The combination of AuNPs and N,Zn-CDs/β-CD shows a detection limit of His around 10 μM, and a selectivity and anti-interference capability for His detection in the present of metal ions, sugar and other biogenic amines. This work exhibits the potential for anti-interference, sensitive and selective detection of His in practical SERS applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Histamine
Surface-enhanced Raman scattering
N,Zn-CDs/β-CD
Fluorescence interference
Detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was greatly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (grant numbers 2019P20173118001 ) and Research on the Key Technology of Vegetable Deep Processing (grant numbers 2019ZG001-4-2 ). Yang Dezhi gratefully acknowledges financial support from Kunming University of Science and Technology high-level talent research platform construction funding (grant numbers 20200097 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra of the N,Zn-CDs/β-CD (a); Raman spectrum of the His, D-His and D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs (b); SEM images of the synthesized AuNPs (c and d).
The Raman signal of D-His+AuNPs, D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs, and His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs (a); The UV–vis spectrum of His, D-His, D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs (b).

The FL sensitization of the N,Zn-CDs/β-CD (a); the FL quenching of the reaction system (b); the SERS enhancement of the system by using the prepared AuNPs as Raman enhancer (c); the influence of AuNPs prepared by different methods on Raman signal (d).

Raman spectra of system with various His concentrations (a); the selectivity of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD to His (b); the anti-interference capability of the proposed method (c); the selectivity of the proposed method for biogenic amines (d).

head.ce:title: Highly selective histamine assay via SERS: Based on the signal enhancement of carbon dots and the fluorescence quenching of gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agro-products Processing, Yunnan Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Yunnan Province 650032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Agro-products Processing, Yunnan Academy of Agricultural Sciences,

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Agro-products Processing, Yunnan Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Yunnan Province 650032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Lunyang Technology Co., Ltd, Yunnan Province 650032, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Lunyang Technology Co., Ltd, Yunnan Province 650032, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent background is a major obstacle in surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and limits its application in various fields of science. This research developed a novel strategy by combining the advantages of the carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to increase the SERS signal for histamine (His) detection. The β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) decorated N,Zn co-doped carbon dots (N,Zn-CDs/β-CD) was prepared, which has both highly selective fluorescence (FL) sensitization and selective Raman signal enhancement for His derivatives (D-His). The prepared AuNPs not only could be used to enhance SERS signal, but also could quench 76% of the FL background at 544 nm, which is conducive to suppress the FL interference caused by carbon dots. Interestingly, the SERS signal increased with the gradual quenching of FL through the energy transfer between the fluorophore and the AuNPs. The combination of AuNPs and N,Zn-CDs/β-CD shows a detection limit of His around 10 μM, and a selectivity and anti-interference capability for His detection in the present of metal ions, sugar and other biogenic amines. This work exhibits the potential for anti-interference, sensitive and selective detection of His in practical SERS applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine as a heterocyclic biogenic amine is formed by the decarboxylation of the amino acid L-histidine, which can be gained from food intake [1,2]. High levels of His can cause a series of clinical manifestations, including nausea, hives, itching, red rash, and diarrhea [3]. There are many reports about the foodborne poisoning caused by His [4,5]. Therefore, the strict regulations for His in various foods is essential for the prevent of safety problems.

The surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is a powerful sensing technology for the His assay owing to their cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, rapid detection, and lack of complicated pretreatment processes [6,7]. Most efforts toward increasing SERS signal have focused upon modulating the nanostructure properties of metal surfaces aimed at generating SERS “hot spots” [8,9]. However, despite the considerable interest and vast potential applications of SERS-based sensors, FL background interference and poor reproducibility are major obstacles to using SERS substrates for quantitative analysis. There is still limited understanding and insufficient control of FL background interference for SERS signals. Because of the much larger cross section of FL of molecules, the FL background might rise strong shot noise for the detection of Raman signal, which becomes a major obstacle in SERS and its application [10].

In past few years, researchers have used FL and Raman to achieve dual-mode detection technology, which utilize FL quenching to achieve FL detection and Raman signal enhancement. The dual-mode FL–SERS method has some advantages regarding the combination of FL and SERS method, which has been used extensively for analysis, bioassays and bioimaging [11–15]. For examples, Sun et al. used the uranyl ions to quench the FL signal of prepared Fe3O4-Au@CdTe; the Raman signal of uranyl was also enhanced after FL quenching [6]. Wang et al. reported that the FL background can be greatly suppressed through the charge transfer between the target molecules and the atomically thin ReS2 [7]. The materials prepared in these reported methods are simultaneously acted as fluorescent probes and SERS substrate. To some extent, SERS phenomena were affected by surface plasmon resonance and charge-transfer [16].

Carbon dots with well optical properties can serve as an excellent electron acceptor and electron donor, and are promising for fluorescent probe and Raman substrate preparation [17]. Considerable effort has been directed toward on the FL quenching and FL sensitization based on CDs [18–22]. There have been a few studies on the Raman enhancement by using CDs [8,9,23]. The FL quenching of CDs makes them very useful for the elimination of FL background and the Raman signal amplification [24,25]. Likewise, combining FL sensitization and Raman may also provide a new way to achieve the Raman signal amplification. The FL sensitization may provide a large conjugation system, which can also further enhance the Raman signal. Another issue is that the enhanced FL interference is a major obstacle in SERS, and the FL need to be suppressed.

In this study, we proposed a novel strategy for SERS measurement of His based on the selective Raman signal amplification of CDs and FL quenching of prepared AuNPs. The prepared CDs showed the selective enhancement of D-His Raman signal through the increase of the conjugate system, which is different from other SERS literatures. In order to suppress the FL interference caused by carbon dots, the AuNPs with dual functions was simultaneously applied for the FL quenching of the system, and as an enhancer to enhance Raman signal. It is interesting that while increasing the amount of as-synthesized AuNPs, the FL of the system gradually decreased, great contrast to the enhancement of the SERS signal. Furthermore, the practical feasibility of the proposed method was studied in real samples.

The N,Zn-CDs/β-CD was synthesized using a microwave method. Firstly, 0.9 g of citric acid, 0.2 g of zinc acetate and 2.6 mL of ethylenediamine was dissolved in deionized water (25 mL). Secondly, the dissolved solution was transferred to the microwave digestion tank and heated at 150 °C for 30 min (850 W). Thirdly, the obtained N,Zn-CDs was purified by filtration through 0.22 µm filter and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. After that, cyclodextrin was used to further modified the carbon dots using the following scheme: 8 mL of N,Zn-CDs was diluted to 15 mL with deionized water, and the pH was adjusted to 4 with HCl (0.1 M). Then, 0.5 g of EDC and 0.5 g of NHS as the cross-linker were added into the solution and then placed for 1 h. When the mixture was heated to 80 °C under stirring, 0.05 g of mono-(6-ethanediamine-6-deoxy)-β-cyclodextrin was added and then kept for 24 h. Finally, when the reaction was finished, the obtained carbon dots (Named as “N,Zn-CDs/β-CD”) was stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

Firstly, carboxymethyl chitosan (CC) (0.1 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water via ultrasound. When the solution was heated to 95 °C, 100 μL of sodium citrate dihydrate was injected under vigorous stirring. Then, 2 mL of HAuCl4 (25 mM) was injected dropwise, and the solution was refluxed for 30 min. During this process, the solution gradually changed from light yellow to purple. Finally, when reaction was finished, the obtained AuNPs colloid was cooled in the ice-water bath and stored in dark at 4 °C for further experiments.

OPA was employed for the derivatization of His (Fig. S1). Briefly, 500 μL of OPA was added into the different concentration of His (12.5 μM–1.25 mM). Then, the solution was adjusted to pH 10 using NaOH (0.1 M) and diluted to 4 mL with deionized water. After placed for 10 min, the D-His is obtained. For FL sensitization, 100 μL of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD was added into the solution. Finally, after placed for 3 min, and the FL spectra of solution were collected with excitation wavelength at 375 nm (slit widths = 5 nm).

After derivatization and sensitization, the His solution was mixed with 300 μL of the prepared AuNPs ( Scheme 1). After shaking, the mixture was placed for 3 min. For SERS measurement, the experiment was performed with a 10 s exposure and three accumulations. The Raman spectrum was collected with a 785 nm laser.

The N,Zn-CDs/β-CD was light yellow transparent liquid with maximum emission and excitation of 352 nm and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. S2). Metal-doped CDs can serve as electron acceptors and donors, and exhibits well photophysical and electronic properties which can make them absorb and emit light [8]. XPS and FTIR spectra were performed to determine the elemental composition and surface functional groups of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD. As shown in Fig. 1a, the N,Zn-CDs/β-CD exhibits four distinct peaks for C1s, N1s, O1s and Zn2p3 at 285.0, 398.6, 530.2 and 1020.0 eV respectively. As can be seen from Fig. S3, the broad peaks at 2960–3500 cm−1 and 2940 cm−1 reveals the existence of O–H and N–H groups. Two peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of CO and C-O/C-N groups, respectively.

Furthermore, the Gaussian 09 programs [density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP/6–31 G(d) level] was used for vibrational frequency calculation of His and D-His. The geometries of His and D-His (Fig. S4) were optimized without no imaginary frequencies. As can been seen from Fig. 1 b, the spectrum of D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs is in good agreement with the standard Raman spectra from D-His. It illustrates several outstanding Raman frequency bands at about 451, 676, 752, 940, 1038 and 1312 cm−1, which corresponds to ring breathing and deformation of benzene ring and nitrogen heterocycle.

In general, gold nanoparticles with excellent optical properties make them easy to modify as the absorption band, which allows them to overlap the FL band of the fluorophore with its absorption band, resulting in the energy transfer between the fluorophore and the AuNPs [26,27]. Therefore, the FL background could be quenched, enabling them to be used to suppress Raman FL background. As can been seen (Inset in Scheme 1, the synthesized AuNPs is wine red and exhibits an absorption peak at 520 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S2), which is consistent with those reported in the literature [28–30]. The morphology and structure of the synthesized AuNPs was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 1c and d, the synthesized AuNPs presents a special dendritic shape without agglomeration, which is conducive to the formation of “hot spots” [31,32].

To investigate whether the His can be directly measured by SERS, the Raman signal of His, D-His, AuNPs, and His+AuNPs system were recorded (Fig. S5). The Raman signal of His could not be observed; after using AuNPs as a Raman enhancer, the Raman signal remains unchanged. These results showed that the His cannot be detected without derivatization. After the His being derivatized using OPA as derivatizing reagent, a small Raman signal could be observed in D-His+AuNPs system ( Fig. 2a). However, the Raman signal was too low and not ideal for His assay. Subsequently, N,Zn-CDs/β-CD as the link reagent was used to modify the His after derivatization. Compared with D-His+AuNPs system, the final results (Fig. 2a) showed that the addition of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD significantly increases the Raman signal. Meanwhile, the Raman signal in His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs system could not be observed (Fig. 2a), which revealed that the derivatization and N,Zn-CDs/β-CD was important for the His assay.

To determine the reaction of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and derivatized His, the UV–vis spectra of His, D-His, D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs were collected (Fig. 2b). After the derivatization, an obvious peak at 376 nm was observed at the spectra of D-His. Then, it is found that when N,Zn-CDs/β-CD was added, the new peak at 389 nm was found in D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD, reveling the formation of a larger conjugate system. Subsequently, it is noticed that a peak positioned at about 530 nm appears in the spectra of D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD+AuNPs, which is mainly caused by the addition of AuNPs. Meanwhile, the peak intensity at 389 nm was also greatly reduced, indicating the interaction between AuNPs and D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD.

Furthermore, the FL intensity before and after the reaction was also investigated ( Fig. 3a). As can been seen, the FL intensity of D-His at 544 nm increased with an increasing amount of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD, indicating that CDs can be effectively linked to D-His. However, His alone cannot change the FL of carbon dots. Following comprehensive comparison of the effects of derivatization and carbon dots on SERS signals, OPA and N,Zn-CDs/β-CD were simultaneously selected to amplify the Raman signal for His detection. Furthermore, the maximum emission wavelength of D-His was at 452 nm; after the addition of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD, an obviously red-shift emission could be observed (Fig. 3a), which is attributable to the reaction between N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and D-His, indicating that a larger conjugated system was formed and the electric field had changed significantly [33–35]. Thus, the interaction between N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and D-His is beneficial to enhance the Raman signal of the system [36].

For SERS measurement, suppressing the FL background is a challenge. To evaluate the effect of the AuNPs, both FL and Raman signal of the system were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3b and c, D-His shows a strong FL intensity at 544 nm while no Raman signal could be observed. However, the FL was suppressed and the Raman signal at 451, 476, 593, 676, 752, 1144 and 1312 cm−1 were distinguishable when the prepared AuNPs was used as Raman enhancer (Fig. 3c). The D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD exhibited a maximal emission and excitation at 543 and 372 nm, respectively (Fig. S5). Fig. S5 displays that the absorption of AuNPs obviously overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectra of D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is likely to be based on the inner filter effect (IFE) between AuNPs and D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD. When the absorption spectrum of the absorber overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum or excitation spectrum of the fluorophore, IFE is due to the absorption of the emitted light and/or excitation light by the absorber [37,38]. Fig. 3a and b shows that, with the increasing addition of AuNPs to the D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD system, the FL intensity of system gradually quenched and the Raman signal of system was significantly enhanced. Fig. S6 shows that the absorption spectral of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD had no significant change with the addition of AuNPs or AuNPs+His, revealing no interaction or complex formation between N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and AuNPs. Furthermore, Fig. 2b shows that the addition of AuNPs exhibited no effect on absorption spectral of D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD system and ruling out interaction or complex formation. Based on the above results, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of AuNPs was attributed to the IFE process between AuNPs and D-His+N,Zn-CDs/β-CD system.

To determine the optimal amount of AuNPs, different volume of AuNPs was added to the system. When the prepared AuNPs was added, the FL intensity at 544 nm significantly decreased with an increasing amount of AuNPs (Fig. 3b). It is noticed that AuNPs quenched the FL of system by the reaction between N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and AuNPs due to electric field change [8]. Meanwhile, as the FL intensity decreases, the Raman signal gradually increases (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3b, the FL intensity at 544 nm is suppressed by 76% when 1200 μL of the prepared AuNPs was added, and Raman signal strength increased from 0 to nearly 40,0000 a.u. at 476 nm (Fig. 3c). These results verified that the prepared AuNPs has a direct influence on FL suppression effect, allowing a possible strategy on augment the Raman signal for His assay.

Furthermore, the AuNPs prepared by different synthetic techniques was also investigated to quench the FL. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, different kinds of AuNPs prepared by different methods, including commercial AuNPs, AuNPs prepared by sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7) [39], AuNPs prepared by sodium borohydride (NaBH4) [40], and AuNPs prepared by CC, showed a slight Raman enhancement effect except the carboxymethyl chitosan-based AuNPs. Although the FL was suppressed, no detectable Raman signals could be found. The above findings implied that as the reducing agent and stabilizer, carboxymethyl chitosan has a significant effect on the interaction between AuNPs and N,Zn-CDs/β-CD. These results suggested that only the prepared AuNPs can enhance the SERS signal while FL interference is avoided.

Based on the signal amplification of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and the FL suppression of AuNPs, we constructed a method for the determination of His. To acquire the lowest detection limit of His, we measured the Raman signals of His with different concentrations, and plotted the relative intensity of the peaks at 476 and 1144 cm−1 as characteristic peaks of His. As shown in Fig. 4a, samples with His concentration larger than 10 μM exhibit obviously Raman signals, indicating that the detection limit for the method is around 10 μM. It is noteworthy that the detection limit is lower than that of national standard GB5009.208—2016 (17.99 μM). The linear correlation for the signal at 476 cm−1 is appropriate for a coefficient range of 10–1200 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9563. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 9.1% for the peaks at 476 and 1144 cm−1. As stated above, it can be concluded from the comparison with national standards and the accuracy of the analysis that the proposed method is adequate to detect His.

The reproducibility of the proposed SERS sensor is essential for evaluating whether the method can be applied in practice. We tested the SERS intensity of His on ten substrates of the same concentration to evaluate the reproducibility. As shown in Fig. S7, the SERS signals of each substrate was basically the same, with an RSD 5.21%, indicating that the method had high reproducibility. In other His Raman studies, His assay is easily achieved through the direct interaction between different SERS substrates and His [41–43]. Compared with other literatures, the proposed approach has higher detection sensitivity and better selectivity due to the signal amplification of carbon dots and the fluorescence quenching effect of AuNPs.

The potential interference for His detection was examined using other different types of metal ions, sugar and biogenic amines, including Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, NO2 -, glucose, sucrose, maltose, cysteine, glycine, glutamine, tryptamine (Try), β-phenethylamine (Phe), tyramine (Tyr), cadaverine (Cad), spermine (Spe), Putrescine (Put) and octamine (Oct). The concentrations of metal ions and sugar were 10 times that of the His, and the control group was an equal volume of deionized water. For other biogenic amines, N,Zn-CDs/β-CD could specifically interact with D-His to produce FL sensitization, indicating the highly selective of anti-interference with other biogenic amines present in samples (Fig. 4b). The FL intensity of the D-His hardly changed in the presence of metal ions and sugar (Fig. S8). Furthermore, only all of the metal ions were mixed with His and the Raman band would be affected (Fig. 4c). Therefore, N,Zn-CDs/β-CD will only interact with D-His, which exhibited a strong anti-interference capability.

To further evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference capability, SERS was also recorded. Only His, putrescine, cadaverine exhibited a certain Raman enhancement, while the spectra of the His was not be disturbed and the Raman band could still be observed at 476 and 1144 cm−1 (Fig. 4d).

The fermented fruit juice and rice wine with spike and recovery experiments was applied for further verification that the proposed His assay probe could be used in practical applications. The SERS signal of the probe had hardly changed (Fig. S9) when fresh juice, including mango juice, grape seed juice, was added, which indicates that the matrix has a negligible impact on the performance of this probe, owing to the specific interaction between N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and D-His. On the contrary, an obviously Raman signal enhancement can be observed in rice wine and fermented grape seed juice (Fig. S9). The recovery values of the three different spiked samples were calculated and are presented in Table S1. The recoveries were between 81.3% and 105.9% with relative standard deviation less than 9.6%. Furthermore, it was clear (Table S2) that the detected His values in actual samples were well consistent with those from the HPLC (Fig. S10). The above results suggested that this SERS detection system can quantify the His level in complicated actual samples with high reliability.

A novel strategy based on the combination of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD and AuNPs was proposed for anti-interference, sensitive, and selective SERS analysis of His. The N,Zn-CDs/β-CD shows highly selective FL sensitization to D-His, which could significantly improve the Raman signal through the increase of the conjugate system. The FL sensitization of N,Zn-CDs/β-CD makes it have good anti-interference and selectivity for His detection. Importantly, the FL background caused by CDs was gradually quenched, while the Raman intensity of His gradually increased with increasing AuNPs volume. Moreover, the His signal would not be influenced in the present of other biogenic amines. The limit of detection for the proposed method was 10 μM, which is promising when compared with national standard method. The proposed Raman method could be employed for detection of His in practical applications, and provided a new strategy for solving Raman FL interference.

Dezhi Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Hong Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Qiulan Li: Validation, Software. Kexiang Li: Investigation. Feijian Xiao: Validation. Yaling Yang: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (grant numbers 2019P20173118001) and Research on the Key Technology of Vegetable Deep Processing (grant numbers 2019ZG001-4-2). Yang Dezhi gratefully acknowledges financial support from Kunming University of Science and Technology high-level talent research platform construction funding (grant numbers 20200097).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130866.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective post-column derivatization coupled to cation exchange chromatography for the determination of histamine and its precursor histidine in fish and Oriental sauce samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Tsiasioti, P.D. Tzanavaras, Selective post-column derivatization coupled to cation exchange chromatography for the determination of histamine and its precursor histidine in fish and Oriental sauce samples, Food Chem. 351 (2021) 129351, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129351.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolorimetric sensing of histamine in fishes based on enzymatic etching of gold nanorods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, X. Wu, Y. Ding, X. Zhou, Multicolorimetric sensing of histamine in fishes based on enzymatic etching of gold nanorods, Food Control 127 (2021) 108143, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2021.108143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive sandwich ELISA using a modified biotin-streptavidin amplified system for histamine detection in fish, prawn and crab

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yang, L. Xu, A.C. P. Dias, X. Zhang, A sensitive sandwich ELISA using a modified biotin-streptavidin amplified system for histamine detection in fish, prawn and crab, Food Chem. 350 (2021) 129196, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Histamine food poisonings: a systematic review and meta-analysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-mode fluorescence–SERS sensor for sensitive and selective detection of uranyl ions based on satellite Fe3O4-Au@CdTe nanostructure

Wang, Dual-mode fluorescence–SERS sensor for sensitive and selective detection of uranyl ions based on satellite Fe3O4-Au@CdTe nanostructure, Sens. Actuators, B 325 (2020) 128644, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128644.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Large-area ReS2 monolayer films on flexible substrate for SERS based molecular sensing with strong fluorescence quenching

Zhao, Large-area ReS2 monolayer films on flexible substrate for SERS based molecular sensing with strong fluorescence quenching, Appl. Surf. Sci. 542 (2021) 148757, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148757.

S.K. Bhunia, L. Zeiri, J. Manna, S. Nandi, R. Jelinek, Carbon-dot/silver-nanoparticle flexible SERS-active films, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2016) 25637–25643, https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.6b10945.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticles/N-doped carbon-dots nanocomposites derived from Siraitia Grosvenorii and its logic gate and surface-enhanced Raman scattering characteristics

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dezhi Yang is a teacher at Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. His research focuses on three primary domains: (a) fluorescent probe and carbon quantum dots, (b) nanozyme and SERS spectra, and (c) environmental and food analysis (such as heavy metals and phenols).

Hong Li is a PhD student at Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. His research focuses on fluorescent probe, nanozyme, and environmental and food analysis.
Qiulan Li is a PhD student at Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. Her research examines the oxidation and degradation properties of materials.
Kexiang Li is a master student at Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. His research focuses on the application of carbon quantum dots in SERS enhancement.

Feijian Xiao is a master student at Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. Her research focuses on the synthesis of carbon quantum dots and its application in analysis.

Yaling Yang is a Professor of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology. She has an interest in analytical chemistry, such as environmental analysis and food analysis. She also has good experience in analytical chemistry.
